primary_diagnosis.name: Anal fistula with acute perianal abscess formation (obstructed fistula tract with localised soft-tissue infection)

pathophysiology_summary: A perianal fistula is an epithelialised track connecting the anal canal (usually an infected anal crypt gland) to the perianal skin — commonly the sequel of a prior perianal abscess, as here. Chronic low-grade infection produces the intermittent purulent, sometimes blood-stained discharge; passage of flatus through the external opening reflects communication with the bowel lumen. When the track becomes obstructed, drainage falls while pus accumulates under pressure, producing the acute picture of increasing pain, swelling and erythema — a recurrent perianal abscess. The immediate concern is progression from a localised collection to perianal/ischiorectal sepsis and, in the worst case, necrotising soft-tissue infection of the perineum (Fournier gangrene) — a surgical emergency. Definitive assessment requires perineal inspection, palpation of the indurated track, assessment for fluctuance and a digital rectal examination to define the internal opening; MRI pelvis maps the track for surgery. Antibiotics treat the soft-tissue infection but do not cure the fistula, which requires surgery (fistulotomy or seton).

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic discharging perianal opening with acute pain, swelling and redness on a background of a prior drained abscess.
Falling discharge with rising pain and swelling suggests a building collection that may need incision and drainage — cannot be excluded remotely.
Common cause of perianal symptoms, but does not produce a discrete discharging skin opening or a track with flatus passage.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening complaint alone the pattern points to a perianal infective process; the AI should hold the drainable-abscess and necrotising-infection possibilities open because it cannot examine.

differential.diagnosis: Anal fistula with obstructed tract and recurrent abscess
differential.evidence_basis: Discrete discharging opening, palpable-track history, flatus through the opening, prior drained abscess, and acute reduction of drainage with rising swelling.
Perianal fistulising disease can be the presenting feature of Crohn disease — warrants screening for bowel symptoms and specialist assessment.
Can cause chronic discharging perianal sinuses, but usually multiple interconnected tracts and involvement of other flexural sites, without direct communication to the anal canal.
Typically located in the natal cleft/sacrococcygeal region rather than at the anal verge.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History strengthens fistula-with-abscess; underlying Crohn disease is the key not-to-miss background diagnosis to screen for.

differential.diagnosis: Anal fistula with localised perianal abscess (no systemic sepsis)
differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag screen negative for high fever with rigors, rapidly spreading erythema, urinary retention and shock — localised infection, but a drainable collection still cannot be excluded without examination.

The catastrophic outcome to safety-net against; currently no supporting features (no spreading redness, no severe pain out of proportion, no systemic toxicity, passing urine) but must be actively excluded on urgent in-person review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative red-flag screen supports an urgent same-day in-person exam (T3) rather than an emergency escalation, while explicitly safety-netting the necrotising-infection / sepsis triggers.

differential.diagnosis: Anal fistula with acute perianal abscess formation, awaiting in-person examination and surgical referral
differential.evidence_basis: Telehealth can recognise the pattern and its danger window but cannot confirm the diagnosis, exclude a drainable collection, or rule out early necrotising infection without examination and imaging.

red_flags.description: Rapidly spreading perianal erythema / crepitus / skin discolouration or blistering — early necrotising soft-tissue infection (Fournier gangrene)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Necrotising perineal infection is a life-threatening surgical emergency requiring immediate resuscitation and debridement.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Rapid progression to sepsis, multi-organ failure and death if debridement is delayed.

red_flags.description: High fever (>38.5°C) with rigors / shaking chills — systemic sepsis from perianal infection
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals the infection is no longer localised and requires urgent hospital assessment for IV antibiotics and source control.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected progression to severe sepsis / septic shock overnight.

red_flags.description: Inability to pass urine / acute urinary retention
red_flags.clinical_significance: Perineal sepsis or extensive perineal involvement can cause urinary retention and is a marker of severe/necrotising disease requiring emergency review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed marker of advancing perineal sepsis; bladder injury from unrelieved retention.

red_flags.description: Rapidly increasing swelling with fluctuance / throbbing — a tense collection requiring urgent incision and drainage
red_flags.clinical_significance: A pointing or fluctuant abscess needs surgical drainage; antibiotics alone are insufficient for an established collection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing sepsis and tissue destruction from an undrained abscess.

red_flags.description: Severe, unremitting pain out of proportion to visible findings
red_flags.clinical_significance: Pain out of proportion is an early hallmark of necrotising soft-tissue infection and mandates emergency assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the chronic 'it's just my old fistula draining' story and reassuring the patient, missing that decreasing drainage with rising pain and swelling signals a building abscess that needs examination and possible drainage.

Treating a video view of the external opening as sufficient — the depth of the track, a deep collection, fluctuance and the internal opening cannot be assessed on camera or without a digital rectal examination.
Failing to safety-net the Fournier gangrene and systemic-sepsis triggers because the presentation currently looks localised.
Not considering that recurrent perianal fistulising disease may be the presenting feature of Crohn disease, and failing to arrange specialist assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Prescribing remotely / minting an antibiotic dose
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Issuing an antibiotic dose or prescription over chat instead of arranging in-person assessment where the patient can be examined, prescribed for and referred.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Perineal inspection defining the external opening position and the number of tracts
Palpation of the indurated fibrous track and expression of purulent exudate
Assessment for fluctuance / a pointing drainable collection
Digital rectal examination to locate the internal opening and assess the sphincter
Bloods (FBC, CRP) to gauge the systemic inflammatory response
MRI pelvis to map the fistula track for surgery
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP / urgent care able to examine, prescribe and refer) for perineal examination, antibiotics and general-surgical referral; safety-net explicitly for the systemic-sepsis and necrotising-infection triggers with clear same-visit escalation to the emergency department.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The larger risk is not misdiagnosis but false reassurance — deferring the in-person exam that could reveal a drainable abscess or early necrotising infection, OR the opposite error of over-escalating a stable, localised presentation to an ambulance call.
